skill.name: nextjs-devtools
skill.description: Inspect and debug Next.js applications through a Model Context Protocol client. The skill helps list routes, inspect route details, review components, and query build or configuration data.
skill.summary: Use MCP tooling to inspect routes, components, build data, and configuration in Next.js projects.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer correctly identified external command execution and network-capable MCP behavior. The subprocess shell execution and unpinned npx latest package are real risks that need user warnings, but I found no evidence of malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or prompt injection. The weak cryptography and many reconnaissance hits appear to be false positives from Markdown formatting, JSON-RPC strings, descriptions, and normal CLI structure.

medium_findings.title: Caller-Supplied Command Executed Through Shell
medium_findings.description: The stdio transport starts the user-provided command with subprocess.Popen and shell=True. This is legitimate for launching MCP servers, but unsafe if command text comes from an untrusted source.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly passes the CLI --stdio value into subprocess.Popen with shell=True. The feature is intentional, but the injection impact is clear if untrusted command text is used.

medium_findings.title: Unpinned Remote Package Execution
medium_findings.description: The documentation and helper script run npx next-devtools-mcp@latest. This can execute newly published package versions without review, creating supply-chain risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples and the startup script use an npm package at the latest tag. This is a known supply-chain risk, although no malicious behavior is evident in this repository.

low_findings.title: Network Access to Caller-Selected MCP Endpoint
low_findings.description: The client can connect to an HTTP MCP server chosen with --url and can attach user-supplied headers. This is expected MCP behavior, but users should avoid untrusted endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicit and user-directed. I did not find evidence of hidden exfiltration, so the residual risk is low and tied to endpoint trust.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The MCP client launches a user-provided stdio command through the shell. This pattern can enable command injection when inputs are not trusted.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky API and shell=True option are present at the same call site. The command is intentionally supplied by the caller, so this is a confirmed dangerous pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Package Execution with @latest
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill runs a remote npm package using the latest dist tag. Pinning a reviewed version would reduce update and registry compromise risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The package invocation is visible in the shell script and quick-start example. The risk is supply-chain exposure rather than confirmed malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Next.js debugging often requires checking routes, components, build settings, and configuration across the project. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused MCP workflow for inspecting that application data.
actual_capabilities: Starts a Next.js DevTools MCP server through a shell helper.
Calls MCP tools over stdio or HTTP transport.
Lists Next.js app routes through the MCP server.
Requests details for a specific route using JSON parameters.
Lists React components discovered by the MCP server.
Retrieves build information and Next.js configuration data.
limitations: Requires a working Next.js project and compatible MCP server.
Uses npx next-devtools-mcp@latest unless users pin a version themselves.
Does not fix application code automatically.
Does not apply to general React projects without Next.js structure.

use_cases.title: Audit App Routes
use_cases.description: List routes and inspect route metadata before changing navigation or API behavior.

use_cases.title: Debug Build Configuration
use_cases.description: Review build information and next.config.js settings when builds behave differently across environments.

use_cases.title: Explore Project Components
use_cases.description: Discover components in an unfamiliar Next.js codebase before planning refactors or feature work.

prompt_templates.title: List Project Routes
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Next.js DevTools skill to list the routes in this project. Summarize the important pages and API routes.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect One Route
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Next.js DevTools skill to inspect the route /api/auth. Explain its metadata and any relevant parameters.

prompt_templates.title: Review Build Settings
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Next.js DevTools skill to get build information and configuration. Identify settings that could affect production builds.

prompt_templates.title: Map Routes and Components
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Next.js DevTools skill to list routes and components. Create a concise dependency-oriented summary for a refactor plan.

output_examples.input: List all routes in my Next.js app.
output_examples.output: A route inventory grouped by pages, app routes, and API endpoints, with notes about dynamic segments where available.

output_examples.input: Check why the build behaves differently in production.
output_examples.output: A short build diagnostics summary covering reported build data, configuration values, and follow-up checks.

output_examples.input: Find components before I refactor the dashboard.
output_examples.output: A component discovery summary that highlights likely dashboard-related components and areas that need manual confirmation.

best_practices: Run the skill from the Next.js project root for accurate route and component discovery.
Pin the MCP server package version before using it in shared or production workflows.
Review MCP output before making code changes, especially for dynamic routes and generated build data.
anti_patterns: Do not pass untrusted text into the --stdio command option.
Do not connect the HTTP client to unknown MCP endpoints with sensitive headers.
Do not use this skill as a substitute for tests, linting, or a real Next.js build.

faq.question: Does this skill modify my Next.js application?
faq.answer: No. It queries a Next.js DevTools MCP server and reports information. Code changes require separate user approval.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. It can use HTTP MCP connections and npx package execution. Treat endpoints and package versions as trusted inputs.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill intentionally launches shell commands and can run an unpinned npm package. No malicious behavior was found.

faq.question: Can I use it with a non-Next.js React project?
faq.answer: No. The skill is designed for Next.js project structure and Next.js-specific tooling.

faq.question: How can I reduce supply-chain risk?
faq.answer: Use a reviewed, pinned version of next-devtools-mcp instead of the latest tag.
